name: Electric Eel
summary: Despite its name, the electric eel is not a true eel, but a species of knifefish that can generate massive electric shocks of up to 860 volts to stun prey, defend itself from predators, and navigate muddy river waters.
metaTitle: Electric Eel Facts | Habitat, Diet, Electricity & Photos
metaDescription: Electric eel facts for kids with photos, Amazon habitat, diet, electric shocks, and anatomy on one easy-to-read page.
funFacts: Despite their name and snakelike appearance, electric eels are actually knifefish, more closely related to carp and catfish than true eels!
An adult electric eel can generate a massive electric shock of up to 860 volts—enough to stun a horse!
They have three special organs in their bodies (the main organ, Hunter's organ, and Sachs' organ) that generate electricity.
Because they live in muddy, low-oxygen waters, electric eels must rise to the surface to breathe air every 10 minutes!
They have very poor eyesight, but they use low-voltage electric pulses like radar to map their surroundings and find food in the dark.
During the dry season, male electric eels build a nest out of their own saliva (spit), where the female lays thousands of eggs.

faq.question: What is an electric eel?
faq.answer: An electric eel is a freshwater fish native to South America. It is famous for its ability to produce powerful electric shocks to hunt, navigate, and defend itself.

faq.question: How does an electric eel generate electricity?
faq.answer: They have thousands of specialized cells called electrocytes stacked together inside three electric organs. When they spot prey or danger, their brain sends a signal that releases a sudden burst of electricity.

faq.question: Is an electric eel a true eel?
faq.answer: No, it is not a true eel. Electric eels are actually a type of knifefish. They are called eels because of their long, cylindrical, and slimy bodies.
